patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extracts the percpu_up_read() slowpath into a new __percpu_up_read() function to avoid binary size increase from adding a tracepoint to an inlined function. The change is made by moving the relevant code from the inline function to a separate function, which is then exported as a symbol.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Moved the percpu_up_read() slowpath out of the inline function into a new __percpu_up_read() to avoid binary size increase from adding a tracepoint to an inlined function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint, contended_release, to track lock hold time under contention. It fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, complementing existing waiter-side tracepoints. The patch modifies slowpath unlock paths for various sleepable locks, including mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, and percpu-rwsem.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to handling contention on rwsem and semaphore locks. Suggested re-evaluating the tracepoint firing order for these cases.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH v3 4/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH v3 4/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This RFC patch proposes extending the contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and rwlocks, aiming to minimize hot path impact by combining contention check, trace call, and unlock in an out-of-line function.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The reviewer has not posted any comments or feedback on the patch yet.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include of linux/sched.h from the tracing/lock code, as none of the trace events reference anything from this header.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new tracepoint, contended_release, which fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released under contention. The goal is to provide more accurate hold time estimation and capture the lock holder's stack at release time.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the contended_release tracepoint firing before the lock is released, which could lead to incorrect results. Suggested reworking the tracepoint placement for consistency.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Provided feedback on the extension of contended_release to queued spinlocks and rwlocks, suggesting a more generic instrumentation approach.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch series aims to simplify the locking mechanism in put_page_back_buddy() by replacing an explicit lock/unlock pattern with a zone lock guard, which automatically cleans up the lock on scope exit.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch introduces a zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy() to replace the explicit lock/unlock pattern, which is a good simplification effort.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series replaces explicit lock/unlock patterns in the mm code with automatic scope-based cleanup using zone locks. The first message introduces this change to __offline_isolated_pages().

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Posted the first message in this thread, introducing a patch series to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup using zone locks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the locking mechanism in the mm module by introducing a zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk(). The current explicit lock/unlock pattern is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup, enhancing code readability and reducing potential bugs.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Posted the initial patch series with a suggested-by and signed-off-by from the author.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the locking mechanism in the page allocation code by introducing a zone lock guard, replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns and allowing for direct return from loops.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the zone lock handling in the mm subsystem, specifically in the set_migratetype_isolate() function. The goal is to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup using a scoped lock guard.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The original author proposed using a scoped lock guard to replace the explicit lock/unlock pattern in set_migratetype_isolate(). The patch aims to improve zone lock handling and reduce the risk of lockdep splats.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the zone lock handling in the mm code by introducing a scope-based cleanup mechanism, replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns and goto statements.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin

review_comments.summary: Suggested simplifying the code by removing unnecessary braces around a single-line if statement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] mm: introduce zone lock guards
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces zone lock guards to simplify the control flow in several mm functions by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup, reducing redundant code and improving maintainability.

reviewer suggested simplifying code by removing unnecessary brackets

You can lose the braces here too:

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt

Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the patch introduces a small overhead in terms of text size and cache footprint, questioning whether this added complexity is justified by the benefits of improved maintainability
review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that zone lock guards are not properly ordered with other locks in the mm functions, specifically mentioning that __offline_isolated_pages() holds the zone lock while also holding the page lock, and requested a reordering of the locks to fix this issue

reviewer questioned whether the introduction of zone lock guards would result in a measurable performance slowdown, and requested data on this aspect

reviewer suggested that the zone lock guard approach be reconsidered in favor of improving the generic locking infrastructure, potentially for performance reasons
review_comments.raw_body:
[ Adding Peter ]

On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

---

I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
Or are we only worried about the text size increase?

---

This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
make the generic infrastructure more performant?

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch introduces a performance regression due to increased lock contention, as evidenced by the increase in get_page_from_freelist() execution time

Reviewer Vlastimil Babka questioned whether the zone lock guard construct is necessary, suggesting that it may be a compiler issue or heuristic, and recommended investigating further rather than rejecting the construct.
review_comments.raw_body:
I get this:

Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6389    6452     +63

---

Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton expressed concern that introducing zone lock guards would impose significant performance overhead on billions of machines, deeming it an unjustified trade-off for developer convenience.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the
time in order to make life a little easier for the developers.  Seems
like a poor tradeoff.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not address the lock ordering issue in vswap_free() and requested a fix

reviewer expressed concern about potential maintenance issues and their impact on a large number of machines
sentiment_signals: impact on a large number of machines
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 10:33:07 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

---

But if there's a bug due to not being as maintainable, that too will affect
billions of machines! To me, that balances the tradeoff.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Peter Zijlstra, pointed out that the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() macro is problematic and suggested replacing it with a conditional version, EXTEND_CLASS_COND(), which would reduce bloat in the kernel.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, so the big problem is __GUARD_IS_ERR(), and that came up before, but
while Linus told me how to fix it, he didn't actually like it very much:

  https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

However it does help with this:

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-post-gcc-16.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 3/2 up/down: 296/-224 (72)
Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6198     +40
free_pcppages_bulk                           678     714     +36
unreserve_highatomic_pageblock               708     736     +28
make_alloc_exact                             280     264     -16
alloc_pages_bulk_noprof                     1415    1399     -16
Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 3/15 up/down: 277/-363 (-86)
Function                                     old     new   delta
unreserve_highatomic_pageblock               708     757     +49
free_pcppages_bulk                           678     707     +29
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6165      +7
try_to_claim_block                          1729    1726      -3
setup_per_zone_wmarks                        656     653      -3
free_pages_prepare                           924     921      -3
calculate_totalreserve_pages                 282     279      -3
alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof             622     619      -3
__free_pages_prepare                         924     921      -3
__free_pages_ok                             1197    1194      -3
__free_one_page                             1330    1327      -3
__free_frozen_pages                         1303    1300      -3
__rmqueue_pcplist                           2786    2777      -9
free_unref_folios                           1905    1894     -11
setup_per_zone_lowmem_reserve                388     374     -14
make_alloc_exact                             280     264     -16
__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof                 5411    5368     -43
nr_free_zone_pages                           189     138     -51
Total: Before=45299, After=45213, chg -0.19%



However, looking at things again, I think we can get rid of that
unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR(), something like the below, Dan?

This then gives:

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 1/16 up/down: 213/-486 (-273)
Function                                     old     new   delta
free_pcppages_bulk                           678     699     +21
try_to_claim_block                          1729    1723      -6
setup_per_zone_wmarks                        656     650      -6
free_pages_prepare                           924     918      -6
calculate_totalreserve_pages                 282     276      -6
alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof             622     616      -6
__free_pages_prepare                         924     918      -6
__free_pages_ok                             1197    1191      -6
__free_one_page                             1330    1324      -6
__free_frozen_pages                         1303    1297      -6
free_pages_exact                             199     183     -16
setup_per_zone_lowmem_reserve                388     371     -17
free_unref_folios                           1905    1888     -17
__rmqueue_pcplist                           2786    2768     -18
nr_free_zone_pages                           189     138     -51
__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof                 5411    5359     -52
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6089     -69
Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%


Anyway, if you all care about the size of things -- those tracepoints
consume *WAAY* more bytes than any of this.


---
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -286,15 +286,18 @@ static __always_inline _type class_##_na
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_#
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true); \
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext, \
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(*_T), \
 		     ({ void *_t = _T; int _RET = (_lock); if (_T && !(_cond)) _t = ERR_PTR(_RET); _t; }), \
 		     class_##_name##_t _T) \
 	static __always_inline void * class_##_name##_ext##_lock_ptr(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
@@ -488,7 +491,7 @@ typedef struct {							\
 static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-	if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock)) { _unlock; }			\
+	if (_T->lock) { _unlock; }					\
 }									\
 									\
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
@@ -565,7 +568,7 @@ __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(_name, _lock)
 
 #define DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond)		\
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true);		\
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext,					\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock),	\
 		     ({ class_##_name##_t _t = { .lock = l }, *_T = &_t;\
 		        int _RET = (_lock);                             \
 		        if (_T->lock && !(_cond)) _T->lock = ERR_PTR(_RET);\

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged that the unconditional usage of zone lock guards could introduce overhead in all kernel builds, agreed to explore improvements to the guard infrastructure to reduce or eliminate this overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look and digging into this.

[...]

---

That's a fair point, but as I understand Andrew's main concern, the
guard() usage becomes part of the code unconditionally, with no way to
disable it, whereas tracepoints can be compiled out. Any overhead
introduced by guards is therefore carried by all kernel builds.

Given that, improvements to the guard infrastructure itself seem worth
exploring regardless of whether this particular patchset ends up going
in. If the overhead can be reduced or eliminated in the common case, it
should make the trade-off much easier.

Thanks again for investigating this and suggesting a possible approach.

review_comments.author: Dan Williams
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that zone lock guards may not be necessary for the reserve_highatomic_pageblock() function, as it already has its own locking mechanism

reviewer suggested delaying the introduction of __GUARD_IS_ERR() until a better version is available, and confirmed that the patch works fine in the CXL subsystem tests
review_comments.raw_body:
Peter Zijlstra wrote:
[..]

---

I think it makes sense, do not make everyone pay the cost of
__GUARD_IS_ERR() at least until a better __GUARD_IS_ERR() comes along.

I gave the below a run through the CXL subsystem tests which uses
conditional guards quite a bit. Worked fine, and looks good to me.

So feel free to add a "tested by" from me. Not putting the actual tag
here so that b4 does not slurp a tag for the wrong patchset.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the zone lock guard introduced in this patch increases code size unnecessarily due to an unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() call, similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(), which generates sub-optimal code.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Author:        Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 09 Mar 2026 17:40:42 +01:00
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Mon, 16 Mar 2026 13:16:49 +01:00

cleanup: Optimize guards

Andrew reported that a guard() conversion of zone_lock increased the
code size unnecessarily.

It turns out the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() is to blame. As
explored earlier [1], __GUARD_IS_ERR(), similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(),
generates somewhat sub-optimal code.

However, looking at things again, it is possible to avoid doing the
__GUARD_IS_ERR() unconditionally. Revert the normal destructors to a
simple NULL test and only add the IS_ERR bit to COND guards.

This cures the reported overhead; as compiled by GCC-16:

	page_alloc.o:

pre:	Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%
post:	Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%

[1] https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net

Reported-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Tested-by: Dan Williams <dan.j.williams@intel.com>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260309164516.GE606826@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net
---
 include/linux/cleanup.h | 19 +++++++++++--------
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/cleanup.h b/include/linux/cleanup.h
index dbc4162..ea95ca4 100644
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -286,15 +286,18 @@ static __always_inline _type class_##_name##_constructor(_init_args)	\
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true); \
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext, \
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(*_T), \
 		     ({ void *_t = _T; int _RET = (_lock); if (_T && !(_cond)) _t = ERR_PTR(_RET); _t; }), \
 		     class_##_name##_t _T) \
 	static __always_inline void * class_##_name##_ext##_lock_ptr(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
@@ -488,7 +491,7 @@ typedef struct {							\
 static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-	if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock)) { _unlock; }			\
+	if (_T->lock) { _unlock; }					\
 }									\
 									\
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
@@ -565,7 +568,7 @@ __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(_name, _lock)
 
 #define DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond)		\
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true);		\
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext,					\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock),	\
 		     ({ class_##_name##_t _t = { .lock = l }, *_T = &_t;\
 		        int _RET = (_lock);                             \
 		        if (_T->lock && !(_cond)) _T->lock = ERR_PTR(_RET);\

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the reserve_highatomic_pageblock() function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code to use a zone lock guard instead of explicit lock/unlock and goto out_unlock patterns. The zone lock guard is a new feature introduced by this patch, which provides automatic scope-based cleanup when exiting the locked region. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of errors due to forgotten unlocks or incorrect locking orders. The patch also updates the include/linux/mmzone_lock.h file to define the zone lock guard and its attributes.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch introduces additional overhead in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() due to the use of the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard, resulting in a 32-byte increase in page_alloc.o text size, and questioned whether this added complexity is justified.
review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard does not properly handle interrupts, suggesting to use a different locking mechanism

Reviewer Steven Rostedt questioned whether using a zone lock guard would introduce a measurable performance slowdown, and asked if maintainability was the sole concern.

reviewer suggested improving the performance of the zone lock guard mechanism, which is being used in multiple kernel locations
review_comments.raw_body:
[ Adding Peter ]

On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

---

I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
Or are we only worried about the text size increase?

---

This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
make the generic infrastructure more performant?

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch introduces a performance regression due to increased function call overhead, citing a perf tool output showing an increase in get_page_from_freelist() calls

Reviewer Vlastimil Babka questioned whether the zone lock guard is actually doing anything different from the original explicit lock/unlock pattern, suggesting that either there's an issue with the infrastructure or compiler heuristics are at play.
review_comments.raw_body:
I get this:

Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6389    6452     +63

---

Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton expressed concern that the patch's use of zone lock guard could have significant performance implications, potentially penalizing billions of machines, and questioned whether this is a worthwhile tradeoff for developer convenience.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the
time in order to make life a little easier for the developers.  Seems
like a poor tradeoff.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard does not properly drop the lock in all error paths, and suggested adding a return statement to ensure the lock is dropped before exiting

Reviewer Steven Rostedt expressed concern about the potential impact of a less maintainable code change on billions of machines, suggesting that the trade-off between maintainability and performance is significant.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 10:33:07 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

---

But if there's a bug due to not being as maintainable, that too will affect
billions of machines! To me, that balances the tradeoff.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra reported difficulty applying the patches to their local kernel trees (Linus' tree and tip), suggesting a potential issue with patch dependencies or versioning.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd love to look into it, but I can't seem to apply these patches to
anything.

By virtue of not actually having the patches, I had to resort to b4, and
I think the incantation is something like:

  b4 shazam cover.1772811429.git.d@ilvokhin.com

but it doesn't want to apply to anything I have at hand. Specifically, I
tried Linus' tree and tip, which is most of what I have at hand.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch series depends on earlier patches in Andrew's mm-new tree and explained why it won't apply cleanly to Linus' tree or tip.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look, Peter.

This series is based on mm-new and depends on my earlier patchset:

https://lore.kernel.org/all/cover.1772206930.git.d@ilvokhin.com/

Those patches are currently only in Andrew's mm-new tree, so this series
won't apply cleanly on Linus' tree or tip.

It should apply on top of mm-new from:

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/akpm/mm

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra noted that the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() in the original patch can be optimized away, and provided an alternative implementation using a conditional EXTEND_CLASS macro to reduce code size.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, so the big problem is __GUARD_IS_ERR(), and that came up before, but
while Linus told me how to fix it, he didn't actually like it very much:

  https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

However it does help with this:

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-post-gcc-16.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 3/2 up/down: 296/-224 (72)
Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6198     +40
free_pcppages_bulk                           678     714     +36
unreserve_highatomic_pageblock               708     736     +28
make_alloc_exact                             280     264     -16
alloc_pages_bulk_noprof                     1415    1399     -16
Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 3/15 up/down: 277/-363 (-86)
Function                                     old     new   delta
unreserve_highatomic_pageblock               708     757     +49
free_pcppages_bulk                           678     707     +29
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6165      +7
try_to_claim_block                          1729    1726      -3
setup_per_zone_wmarks                        656     653      -3
free_pages_prepare                           924     921      -3
calculate_totalreserve_pages                 282     279      -3
alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof             622     619      -3
__free_pages_prepare                         924     921      -3
__free_pages_ok                             1197    1194      -3
__free_one_page                             1330    1327      -3
__free_frozen_pages                         1303    1300      -3
__rmqueue_pcplist                           2786    2777      -9
free_unref_folios                           1905    1894     -11
setup_per_zone_lowmem_reserve                388     374     -14
make_alloc_exact                             280     264     -16
__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof                 5411    5368     -43
nr_free_zone_pages                           189     138     -51
Total: Before=45299, After=45213, chg -0.19%



However, looking at things again, I think we can get rid of that
unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR(), something like the below, Dan?

This then gives:

$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc-pre-gcc-16.o defconfig-build/mm/page_alloc.o | grep -v __UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 1/16 up/down: 213/-486 (-273)

alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof             622     616      -6

__free_pages_ok                             1197    1191      -6
__free_one_page                             1330    1324      -6
__free_frozen_pages                         1303    1297      -6

setup_per_zone_lowmem_reserve                388     371     -17
free_unref_folios                           1905    1888     -17

nr_free_zone_pages                           189     138     -51
__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof                 5411    5359     -52
get_page_from_freelist                      6158    6089     -69
Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%


Anyway, if you all care about the size of things -- those tracepoints
consume *WAAY* more bytes than any of this.


---
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -286,15 +286,18 @@ static __always_inline _type class_##_na
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_#
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true); \
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext, \
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(*_T), \
 		     ({ void *_t = _T; int _RET = (_lock); if (_T && !(_cond)) _t = ERR_PTR(_RET); _t; }), \
 		     class_##_name##_t _T) \
 	static __always_inline void * class_##_name##_ext##_lock_ptr(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
@@ -488,7 +491,7 @@ typedef struct {							\
 static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-	if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock)) { _unlock; }			\
+	if (_T->lock) { _unlock; }					\
 }									\
 									\
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
@@ -565,7 +568,7 @@ __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(_name, _lock)
 
 #define DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond)		\
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true);		\
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext,					\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock),	\
 		     ({ class_##_name##_t _t = { .lock = l }, *_T = &_t;\
 		        int _RET = (_lock);                             \
 		        if (_T->lock && !(_cond)) _T->lock = ERR_PTR(_RET);\

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, but did not provide a clear plan for addressing Peter's concern about the lock ordering.

Author acknowledged that the use of zone lock guard could introduce overhead in all kernel builds, but suggests exploring improvements to the guard infrastructure to mitigate this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look and digging into this.

[...]

---

That's a fair point, but as I understand Andrew's main concern, the
guard() usage becomes part of the code unconditionally, with no way to
disable it, whereas tracepoints can be compiled out. Any overhead
introduced by guards is therefore carried by all kernel builds.

Given that, improvements to the guard infrastructure itself seem worth
exploring regardless of whether this particular patchset ends up going
in. If the overhead can be reduced or eliminated in the common case, it
should make the trade-off much easier.

Thanks again for investigating this and suggesting a possible approach.

review_comments.author: Dan Williams
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Dan Williams noted that the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard does not properly handle the case where reserve_highatomic_pageblock() is called from an interrupt context, and suggested using a different locking mechanism to avoid potential deadlocks.

Reviewer Dan Williams suggested using __GUARD_IS_ERR() only when a better implementation is available, and confirmed the patch works fine in the CXL subsystem tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Peter Zijlstra wrote:
[..]

---

I think it makes sense, do not make everyone pay the cost of
__GUARD_IS_ERR() at least until a better __GUARD_IS_ERR() comes along.

I gave the below a run through the CXL subsystem tests which uses
conditional guards quite a bit. Worked fine, and looks good to me.

So feel free to add a "tested by" from me. Not putting the actual tag
here so that b4 does not slurp a tag for the wrong patchset.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the recent cleanup patch optimized guards by reverting to a simple NULL test for destructors, and suggested applying this optimization to the zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to avoid unnecessary code size increase.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Author:        Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 09 Mar 2026 17:40:42 +01:00
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Mon, 16 Mar 2026 13:16:49 +01:00

cleanup: Optimize guards

Andrew reported that a guard() conversion of zone_lock increased the
code size unnecessarily.

It turns out the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() is to blame. As
explored earlier [1], __GUARD_IS_ERR(), similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(),
generates somewhat sub-optimal code.

However, looking at things again, it is possible to avoid doing the
__GUARD_IS_ERR() unconditionally. Revert the normal destructors to a
simple NULL test and only add the IS_ERR bit to COND guards.

This cures the reported overhead; as compiled by GCC-16:

	page_alloc.o:

pre:	Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%
post:	Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%

[1] https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net

Reported-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Tested-by: Dan Williams <dan.j.williams@intel.com>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260309164516.GE606826@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net
---
 include/linux/cleanup.h | 19 +++++++++++--------
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/cleanup.h b/include/linux/cleanup.h
index dbc4162..ea95ca4 100644
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -286,15 +286,18 @@ static __always_inline _type class_##_name##_constructor(_init_args)	\
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true); \
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext, \
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(*_T), \
 		     ({ void *_t = _T; int _RET = (_lock); if (_T && !(_cond)) _t = ERR_PTR(_RET); _t; }), \
 		     class_##_name##_t _T) \
 	static __always_inline void * class_##_name##_ext##_lock_ptr(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
@@ -488,7 +491,7 @@ typedef struct {							\
 static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-	if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock)) { _unlock; }			\
+	if (_T->lock) { _unlock; }					\
 }									\
 									\
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
@@ -565,7 +568,7 @@ __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(_name, _lock)
 
 #define DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond)		\
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true);		\
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext,					\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock),	\
 		     ({ class_##_name##_t _t = { .lock = l }, *_T = &_t;\
 		        int _RET = (_lock);                             \
 		        if (_T->lock && !(_cond)) _T->lock = ERR_PTR(_RET);\
